section.heading: Introduction
section.paragraph: XML (Extensible Markup Language) is a versatile markup language that allows developers to define their own tags. One of its powerful features is mixed content.
Mixed content occurs when an element contains both text and child elements. This is common in documents like HTML, XHTML, and other structured document formats.

section.heading: Why Use Mixed Content?

list.item: Marking up semantic content within prose

section.paragraph: Consider this example where we have text with emphasis, strong, and inline code all within the same paragraph.

section.heading: Technical Details
section.paragraph: According to the XML 1.0 Specification, mixed content is declared in the DTD as:

list.item:
strong - Bold text
code - Code snippets

section.heading: Best Practices
section.paragraph: When working with mixed content in XML:

list.item: Use meaningful element names that describe semantics

Maintain consistency in your content model
Consider using attributes for metadata

blockquote.paragraph: "The power of XML lies in its flexibility to represent structured data in a human-readable format."

section.heading: Conclusion
section.paragraph: Mixed content is a fundamental concept in XML that enables rich document authoring. By understanding how to properly model mixed content, developers can create more expressive and semantically meaningful XML documents.
For more information, visit our documentation or contact us at support@example.com.
